Based on the retrieved evidence, I agree with the initial reasoning that bilateral TMJ ankylosis can contribute to obstructive sleep apnea by limiting mandibular movement, potentially causing posterior displacement of the tongue and narrowing of the pharynx. However, it is less common compared to well-established factors like micrognathia or retrognathia, which directly reduce upper airway size.
